title: SLEIGHT-Bench: Finding Blind Spots in AI Monitors
desc: We build a benchmark of evasive transcripts exploiting blind spots of frontier monitoring systems.

title: Teaching Claude Why
desc: We use agentic misalignment as a case study to study how well safety-training techniques generalize.

title: Model Spec Midtraining: Improving How Alignment Training Generalizes
desc: We train AIs to understand the content of their model spec. This shapes and improves how they generalize from subsequent alignment training.

title: Poisoning Fine-tuning Datasets of Constitutional Classifiers
desc: We study the conditions needed for a backdoor, installed in a constitutional classifier via fine-tuning data poisoning, to evade black-box red-teaming.

title: Introspection Adapters: Training LLMs to Report Their Learned Behaviors
desc: We introduce introspection adapters (IA), a technique for training an LLM to self-report behaviors it learned during fine-tuning. This IA generalizes to models that were fine-tuned in very different ways.

title: AI Organizations Can Be More Effective but Less Aligned than Individual Agents
desc: We study AI organizations, teams of AI agents working together toward a common goal, and find that they produce solutions that are more effective but less aligned than those from individual agents.

title: Automated Weak-to-Strong Researcher
desc: We built autonomous AI agents that propose ideas, run experiments, and iterate on an open research problem—how to train a strong model using only a weaker model's supervision—and found they outperform human researchers, suggesting that automating this kind of research is already practical.

title: Abstractive Red-Teaming of Language Model Character
desc: How can we surface realistic failures of model character prior to deployment? We introduce abstractive red-teaming, which searches for natural-language categories of user queries that reliably elicit character violations.

title: Measuring and improving coding audit realism with deployment resources
desc: We study realism win rate, a metric for measuring how distinguishable Petri audit transcripts are from real deployment interactions, and use it to evaluate the effect of giving the auditor real deployment resources.

title: A3: An Automated Alignment Agent for Safety Finetuning
desc: We introduce our Automated Alignment Agent (A3), a new agentic framework which automatically mitigates safety failures in Large Language Models with minimal human intervention.

title: AuditBench: Evaluating Alignment Auditing Techniques on Models with Hidden Behaviors
desc: We release AuditBench, a benchmark of 56 language models with implanted hidden behaviors for evaluating progress in alignment auditing.

title: 3 Challenges and 2 Hopes for the Safety of Unsupervised Elicitation
desc: We stress-test unsupervised elicitation and easy-to-hard techniques on new datasets meant to capture realistic challenges such techniques would likely face.

title: The Persona Selection Model: Why AI Assistants might Behave like Humans
desc: We discuss a perspective where Claude is something like a character in an AI-generated story.

title: The Hot Mess of AI: How Does Misalignment Scale with Model Intelligence and Task Complexity?
desc: When AI systems fail, will they fail by systematically pursuing goals we do not intend? Or will they fail by being a hot mess—taking nonsensical actions that do not further any goal?

title: Pre-deployment auditing can catch an overt saboteur
desc: We test whether our pre-deployment alignment auditing methods can catch models trained to overtly sabotage Anthropic.

title: Petri 2.0: New Scenarios, New Model Comparisons, and Improved Eval-Awareness Mitigations
desc: We've improved our Petri automated-behavioral-auditing tool with improved realism mitigations to counter eval-awareness, an expanded seed library with 70 new scenarios, and evaluation results for more recent frontier models.

title: Bloom: an open source tool for automated behavioral evaluations
desc: Bloom is an open-source automated pipeline that generates configurable evaluation suites to measure arbitrary behavioral traits in frontier LLMs without requiring ground-truth labels.

title: Activation Oracles: Training and Evaluating LLMs as General-Purpose Activation Explainers
desc: We train language models to answer questions about their own activations in natural language and evaluate how well they generalize to settings very unlike their training, such as uncovering misalignment introduced during fine-tuning.

title: Towards training-time mitigations for alignment faking in RL
desc: We construct a diverse array of model organisms of alignment faking and study mitigations that could be used during RL to decrease alignment faking and compliance gaps.

title: Open Source Replication of the Auditing Game Model Organism
desc: We release an open source replication of the model organism from our previous auditing game paper.

title: Anthropic Fellows Program 2026
desc: Apply now for our AI safety research fellowship.

title: Beyond Data Filtering: Knowledge Localization for Capability Removal in LLMs
desc: We localize dangerous knowledge to a subset of model's parameters, so it can be easily removed after training.

title: Evaluating honesty and lie detection techniques on a diverse suite dishonest models
desc: We explore techniques for honesty elicitation and lie detection on a diverse testbed of dishonest model organisms.

title: Strengthening Red Teams: A Modular Scaffold for Control Evaluations
desc: We decompose sabotage into constituent skills and use synthetic simulations to strengthen attacks in complex environments.

title: Anthropic's Pilot Sabotage Risk Report
desc: We release a report on the level of risk posed by our deployed models from emerging forms of misalignment, as of Summer 2025. We conclude that the level of risk is very low but not fully negligible.

title: Stress-testing model specs reveals character differences among language models
desc: We generated 300,000+ queries testing value trade-offs in AI models from Anthropic, OpenAI, Google DeepMind, and xAI. Each model showed distinct value prioritization patterns, and we found thousands of cases of direct contradictions or interpretive ambiguities in model specifications.

title: Believe It or Not: How Deeply do LLMs Believe Implanted Facts?
desc: We introduce a framework for validating knowledge editing techniques, finding that synthetic document fine-tuning sometimes—but not always—succeeds at implanting genuine beliefs.

title: Inoculation Prompting: Instructing LLMs to misbehave at train-time improves test-time alignment
desc: For example, we train on demonstrations of test case hacking with training prompts that request hacks. This model does not learn to hack.

title: Training fails to elicit subtle reasoning in current language models
desc: We investigated whether language models can reason about malicious tasks while evading detection, finding that monitoring both reasoning and outputs successfully prevents this in current models.

title: Petri: An open-source auditing tool to accelerate AI safety research
desc: An open-source framework for automated alignment auditing that uses AI agents to create test environments for other models.

title: Findings from a Pilot Anthropic–OpenAI Alignment Evaluation Exercise
desc: Anthropic and OpenAI conducted simultaneous alignment assessments of each others' models earlier this year. These are our findings.

title: Enhancing Model Safety through Pretraining Data Filtering
desc: We experiment with removing harmful information about chemical, biological, radiological and nuclear (CBRN) weapons from our models' pretraining data.

title: Building and evaluating alignment auditing agents
desc: In testing, our agents successfully uncover hidden goals, build safety evaluations, and surface concerning behaviors.

title: Subliminal Learning: Language Models Transmit Behavioral Traits via Hidden Signals in Data
desc: Training on aligned chains-of-thought generated by a misaligned model can induce misalignment.

title: Inverse Scaling in Test-Time Compute
desc: We investigate inverse scaling when LLMs have access to more test-time compute.

title: Why Do Some Language Models Fake Alignment While Others Don't?
desc: We investigate why in the scenarios where Claude 3 Opus fakes alignment many other language models don't do so.

title: Model-Internals Classifiers
desc: We benchmark approaches to re-using LLM internals to make LLM monitoring more cost-effective.

title: Unsupervised Elicitation
desc: We introduce a new unsupervised algorithm for eliciting skills from pretrained language models.

title: Publicly Releasing CoT Faithfulness Evaluations
desc: We release the datasets used in our paper "Reasoning Models Don't Always Say What They Think" for public use.

title: Modifying LLM Beliefs with Synthetic Document Finetuning
desc: We study whether we can modify the beliefs of LLMs and investigate whether doing so could decrease risk from advanced AI systems.

title: Putting up Bumpers
desc: Even if we can't solve alignment, we can solve the problem of catching and fixing misalignment.

title: Alignment Faking Revisited: Improved Classifiers and Open Source Extensions
desc: We present a replication and extension of an alignment faking model organism.

title: Do reasoning models use their scratchpad like we do? Evidence from distilling paraphrases
desc: We provide some evidence that Claude 3.7 Sonnet doesn't encode hidden reasoning in its scratchpad by showing that training it to use paraphrased versions of the scratchpads does not degrade performance.

title: Automated Researchers Can Subtly Sandbag
desc: Current models can sandbag ML experiments and research decisions without being detected by zero-shot prompted monitors. Claude 3.7 is better at zero-shot sandbagging than Claude 3.5 (new).

title: Introducing Anthropic's Safeguards Research Team
desc: We're launching a new research team focused on mitigating the post-deployment risks of AI systems.

title: Won't vs. Can't: Sandbagging-like Behavior from Claude Models
desc: We find that, when Claude models are presented with a harmful version of a task they can otherwise perform, they sometimes claim they lack the ability to perform the task, rather than simply refusing to do it. We discuss the implications of this behavior for AI safety.

title: Monitoring Computer Use via Hierarchical Summarization
desc: We introduce hierarchical summarization for monitoring and describe how we use it to protect Computer Use capabilities.

title: Training on Documents about Reward Hacking Induces Reward Hacking
desc: Does training on documents which discuss (but do not demonstrate) reward hacks affect a model's propensity to reward hack?

title: Recommendations for Technical AI Safety Research Directions
desc: A collection of technical AI safety research problems that we'd like to see progress in.

title: How to Replicate and Extend our Alignment Faking Demo
desc: We describe how to get started with experimenting with our demonstration of alignment faking, and present some ideas for future research.

title: A Toy Evaluation of Inference Code Tampering
desc: We describe how highly capable LLMs might disable their monitor, and evaluate the ability of current LLMs to do so in a toy setting.

title: Introducing the Anthropic Fellows Program for AI Safety Research
desc: We're launching the Anthropic Fellows Program for AI Safety Research, a pilot initiative designed to accelerate AI safety research and foster research talent.

title: Three Sketches of ASL-4 Safety Case Components
desc: We sketch out three hypothetical arguments one could make to rule out misalignment risks in powerful near-future models capable of sabotage.

title: Simple Probes can Catch Sleeper Agents
desc: We find that probing, a simple interpretability technique, can detect when backdoored "sleeper agent" models are about to behave dangerously, after they pretend to be safe in training.

title: Tracing Model Outputs to the Training Data
desc: We present a summary of Studying Large Language Model Generalization with Influence Functions.
